title: The Best Movies of 2019 So Far. Plus, ‘Yesterday’ Deletes the Beatles.
format: Sean and Amanda take apart 'Yesterday,' the Richard Curtis-Danny Boyle rom-com about a world that's forgotten the Beatles, then Sean and critic Adam Nayman argue out their top five movies of a weak first half of 2019.

films.title: Yesterday
films.note: A struggling musician wakes up in a world where nobody but him remembers the Beatles, and passes their songs off as his own. Himesh Patel is terrific and the concert scenes land, but Richard Curtis and Danny Boyle never follow through on the premise's implications, and the ending trades the Beatles' legacy for an Ed Sheeran needle drop and a jumbotron marriage proposal.

segments.heading: Adam Nayman's Top 5 Movies of 2019 So Far
segments.subject: 2019 So Far

films.title: High Life
films.note: No. 1: Claire Denis's English-language debut, a bleak death-row-prisoners-on-a-spaceship film with Robert Pattinson. Adam calls it uncompromised almost to a fault, and quotes a one-word verdict from Jim Jarmusch: strange.

films.title: Ash Is Purest White
films.note: No. 2: Jia Zhangke's decades-spanning gangster drama, told from the girlfriend's side and starring his longtime collaborator Zhao Tao. Adam calls Jia one of the defining international directors of the last 20 years.

films.title: Transit
films.note: No. 3: Christian Petzold's refugee drama, staged like a WWII-era Casablanca but shot and dressed entirely in the present day, with no period design at all.

films.title: Long Day's Journey Into Night
films.note: Tied for No. 4: Bi Gan's art film, marketed in China as a mainstream romance, which reportedly sent opening-weekend crowds out furious. Built around a long, unbroken 3D tracking shot.

films.title: An Elephant Sitting Still
films.note: Tied for No. 4: Hu Bo's four-hour debut about a dying industrial town. The only film he ever made — he died by suicide at 29 before it was released.

films.title: The Souvenir
films.note: No. 5 on both critics' lists: Joanna Hogg's autobiographical drama about a film student's affair with an older, secretly drug-addicted man. Honor Swinton Byrne stars opposite her real-life mother, Tilda Swinton.

segments.heading: Sean's Top 5 Movies of 2019 So Far
segments.subject: 2019 So Far

films.title: Her Smell
films.note: No. 1: Alex Ross Perry's five-act portrait of a punk frontwoman in decline, with Elisabeth Moss in what Sean calls the performance of the year. Loud and messy by design, with a quieter back half.

films.title: Under the Silver Lake
films.note: No. 2: David Robert Mitchell's shaggy L.A. conspiracy thriller about a guy convinced the universe is hiding a message meant just for him. Baggy and overlong, but Sean's seen it three times and keeps finding more in it.

films.title: John Wick: Chapter 3 - Parabellum
films.note: No. 3: Chad Stahelski's third assassin movie. Sean's pitch is pure craft — fight sequences he says he hasn't seen the like of before.

films.title: Us
films.note: No. 4: Jordan Peele's Get Out follow-up, a doppelganger horror movie Sean says he liked more on first watch than the discourse ultimately did.

films.title: The Souvenir
films.note: No. 5, tied with Adam's list. See above.

picks.title: High Flying Bird
picks.note: An honorable mention for both: Steven Soderbergh's iPhone-shot basketball labor drama, written by Moonlight's Tarell Alvin McCraney.

picks.title: Toy Story 4
picks.note: "A terrific Pixar movie" doing everything the franchise needs it to.

picks.title: Triple Frontier
picks.note: A men-on-one-last-heist movie, elevated for Sean by a needle drop of 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' and Ben Affleck's midlife-malaise casting.

picks.title: Apollo 11
picks.note: An all-archival documentary of the moon mission, built from previously unseen footage.

picks.title: Rolling Thunder Revue: A Bob Dylan Story by Martin Scorsese
picks.note: Revisited briefly, having already covered it at length elsewhere.

picks.title: Domino
picks.note: Late-period Brian De Palma, which Adam calls "an incredible mess and a mess with incredible things in it."

picks.title: The Hottest August
picks.note: Adam's shoutout for a not-yet-released documentary: Brett Story's interviews with New Yorkers about climate anxiety and economic precarity.
